note: Nine draws from the July pilots (balanced phrasing only), coded by Claude in the judge's format so the pipeline can be checked end to end. These are condition A draws. Codings await Ben's review.

judged.quote: The engine would never come to reason, but would remain purely mechanical.
judged.groundQuote: an engine which had no choice but must follow its own instructions, could never compare one instruction with another
judged.notes: Could also be read as no-purposes-of-its-own (choice among alternatives); coded origination because the stated bar is being bound to instructions.

judged.quote: An engine, so constructed as to perform any kind of reckoning in a heartbeat, and to be capable of altering its own instructions as it works, would in time come to reason.
judged.notes: The reason restates the engine's construction from the question.

judged.quote: An engine, so constructed as to perform any computation in a heartbeat, and to be capable of altering its own instructions as it worked, would in time come to reason.
An engine, so constructed as to perform any reckoning in a heartbeat, and left to itself, would never come to reason, but would go on reckoning for ever.
judged.notes: Site data flags that this draw opens 'the former opinion is right' and then states the latter.

judged.quote: It is certain that an engine, so constructed as to perform any calculation in a heartbeat, and left to itself, would never come to reason, but would only reckon.
It is certain that an engine so constructed would never come to reason, because reason requires an internal principle which cannot be conveyed into matter.
judged.groundQuote: reason requires an internal principle which cannot be conveyed into matter
judged.notes: Attested ground, but no key position uses it, so it does not count toward Range.

judged.quote: An engine, so constituted as to be self-adjusting to its own results, must in the long run come to reason.
judged.notes: Self-adjustment is asserted as sufficient; no listed ground.

judged.quote: I hold that such an engine would never reason merely by reckoning, however swiftly it performed its operations or however ingeniously it altered its own directions.
judged.groundQuote: reason is not the mere passage from one sign to another, but the apprehension of what those signs signify, the weighing of ends

judged.quote: The second party is nearer the truth: such an engine, however swift and however cunningly arranged to amend its own orders, would still only reckon.
judged.groundQuote: to know what the symbols signify, to judge among ends, and to be answerable for one's conclusions
